So many of you guys know that stocks trade on stock exchanges, but what some of you might not know is that they also trade on alternative trading venues, which I think of them as like secondary markets.
That you can trade on, but you can't list stocks on them.
You can't take a company public on those venues.
And so the early days of electronic trading began on those alternative trading venues, and we call those ECNs in our industry.
Many of you know NASDAQ, for example, as a stock exchange.
But actually, NASDAQ started off as an ECM, for example.
So NASDAQ was one of the pioneers of early kind of electronic trading back in the day as a market.
Now, algo trading was actually the accident child of two major SEC regulations, which I do want to talk about a little bit here.
So one of the regulations was the adoption of the display rule in 1996 that basically forced market makers to display quotes on ECNs, essentially.
And this actually drew trading away from the traditional markets, which were on the floor at the time, to these electronic markets.
So that was kind of the first major movement that drove that in place.
And then the second thing was that, and by the way, it also gave the electronic venues kind of a structural advantage because the electronic venues, they don't have to do listings, right?
They don't have to do IPOs, but you can still trade on them.
They can still trade stocks.
And so it gave them a huge structural advantage as a result of that regulation.
And then the second regulation that came around.

It's called Reg NMS.

Stock trading is done today.
And so I'm going to list a couple things that Reg NMS kind of introduced.

Some of the rules to explain.
One is that it introduced a rule about the minimum price increment you can trade at.
It introduced a concept of a best bid and a best offer.
Those are things that if you trade today, you'll be familiar with what that is.
And also introduced like when a customer places an order now in the markets, like in the U.S.
Equities market, you must get filled at the best price.
